content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Dwight L. Moore 
Town Attorney 
5555 Skyway 
Paradise, CA 95969-4931 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-175 
Dear Mr. Moore: 
October 13, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. Please also note that we are not a finder of 
fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit three members of the Paradise Town 
Council from participating in decisions concerning the fonnation of a proposed sewer assessment 
district? 
CONCLUSION 
As explained below, although all three members have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the Act, the public generally exception applies to allow two of the members to participate in 
such decisions. 
FACTS 
You are the Town Attorney for the Town of Paradise. You seek advice concerning the Act's 
conflict of interest provisions on behalf of Mayor Jody Jones and Councilmembers Greg Bolin and 
Scott Lotter with respect to decisions concerning the fonnation of a proposed sewer assessment 
district that would serve specific commercial real property areas within the Town. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless other.vise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections I 8110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless other.vise indicated. 


File No. A-16-175 
Page No. 2 
The Town of Paradise is governed by a Town Council with five Town-wide elected 
members. The Town does not provide any centralized wastewater treatment services to real 
properties within the Town limits. Improved real properties are served by private septic systems. 
The Town is currently conducting a comprehensive sewer wastewater study pertaining to 
various options relating to the treatment of wastewater within proposed commercial areas as shown 
on the map you provided. The study areas include more than 90% of the jurisdiction's commercial 
real property. In order to implement the selected option, it may be necessary for the Town Council 
to form a sewer assessment district for a Town wastewater public utility service to the properties 
within the district. There would most likely be sewer connection and operating fees that would need 
to be adopted by the Town Council. 
Mayor Jones, Councilmember Bolin and Councilmember Lotter each have an interest worth 
more than $2,000 in commercial real property within the proposed sewer assessment district: 
• Mayor Jones owns an office building consisting of 3,000 square feet with adjacent 
parking. Her spouse owns and operates Farmers Insurance Agency at the location. The 
building also has three tenants who lease smaller suites. There are currently no 
wastewater problems at this property. 
• Councilmember Greg Bolin owns three commercial real properties in the proposed 
sewer assessment district: 
1) Ponderosa Professional Center - a professional office complex with a total of 
27,729 square feet within ten buildings; 
2) Cobblestone Court - a professional office complex with a total of 26,452 square 
feet within six buildings; and 
3) Acorn Oaks Mini Storage- a storage business with a total of 57,870 square feet 
consisting of 367 storage units on 12 acres. 
• Councilmember Scott Lotter leases 4.5 acres on which he operates a multi-screen movie 
theater with parking. 
There are currently no wastewater problems at any of the above real properties. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section 
87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87103 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the 
interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. These interests are: 
. 


File No. A-16-175 
Page No. 3 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment2 worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 
87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 
87103(b).) 
3. Any source of income,3 except gifts or loans by a commercial lending 
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to 
the public without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred 
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103(d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or 
gifts aggregating [$460] or more in value provided to, received by, or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
Mayor Jones and Councilmcmbcr Bolin 
Financial Interest 
Mayor Jones has an interest in her commercial real property as the facts concede her interest 
is worth $2,000 or more. She also has an interest in her spouse's business entity assuming such 
interest is worth $2,000 or more. Lastly, she has an interest in any tenants leasing her property as 
sources of income who paid her $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision. 
Council member Bolin has an interest in all three of his commercial real properties as the 
facts concede his interest in each is worth $2,000 or more. He also has an interest in any tenants 
leasing his properties as sources of income who paid him more $500 or more in the 12 months prior 
to the decision. 
 "Indirect investment" is defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section 
87103.) 
3 "Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata 
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, 
or beneficially, a IO-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) 


Foreseeabili(v and Materiality 
File No. A-16-175 
Page No. 4 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in 
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved in a decision include an interest that is a 
named party in, or subject of, a government decision, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). (Regulation 
18701 (a).) The commercial real property interests of Mayor Jones and Councilmember Bolin are 
explicitly involved in the governmental decisions, and the financial effect is material, because the 
decisions will impose an assessment or fees on the properties. (See Regulation 18702.2(a)(3).) If a 
foreseeable financial effect is material, a conflict exists under the Act and Mayor Jones and 
Councilmember Bolin may not participate absent any exceptions.°' 
Councilmcmber Lotter 
Fi11a11cial Interest 
Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or 
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction 
owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her 
immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
Interests in real property of an individual include a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or 
beneficially, a IO-percent interest or greater." Councilmember Lotter has an interest in his leasehold 
for his movie theatre business because, as your facts state, this interest is worth $2,000 or more. In 
addition, he has an interest in the movie theatre as a business entity assuming such interest is worth 
$2,000 or more. 
Foreseeability a11d Materiality 
Unlike the real property interests of Mayor Jones and Councilmember Bolin, 
Councilmember Lotter's leasehold interest is not explicitly involved in the decisions in question. 
Thus, pursuant to Regulation 1870 l (b ), if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect 
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot 
be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not 
reasonably foreseeable. 
For the property interest in a leasehold interest, the materiality standards are provided in 
Regulation 18702.2(b). Under this regulation, a financial effect on a leasehold interest is 
foreseeable if the decision will, among other things, "[i]ncrease or decrease the potential rental 
value of the property." (Regulation l 8702.2(b )(2).5 
4 We note here, however, that the proposed sewer assessment district would not have a foreseeable and 
material financial effect on the interests ofCouncilmember Lotter or Mayor Jones in their business entities or sources of 
income under Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.3, respectively. Therefore, these interests are not analyzed further. 
5 This factor now considers the potential rental value rather than the actual amount of rent paid by the lessee 
(the factor under fonner Regulation 18705.2(a)(2)(B)). 


File No. A-16-175 
Page No. 5 
Your facts state that the City Council will be considering whether to fonn a sewer 
assessment district to serve specific commercial real properties, including the real property where 
Councilmember Lotter currently has a leasehold interest. If the district is fonned, the affected real 
property owners will most likely experience increased costs as a result of sewer connection and 
operating fees. There is certainly a realistic possibility that these increased costs resulting from an 
improvement to the sewer system that will serve the affected real properties, will increase the 
overall rental values of such properties. Accordingly, Councilmember Lotter may not participate in 
the subject decisions absent any exceptions. 
Public General(v Exception 
Regulation 18703 provides: 
(a) General Rule. A governmental decision's financial effect on a public 
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is 
affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared 
to the effect on the significant segment. 
As pertinent to your facts, a significant se!,rment of the public is at least 25 percent of"[a]II 
real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's 
jurisdiction" (Regulation 18703(b)(2).) Accordingly, the proposed sewer assessment district will 
affect 90% of commercial real properties in the Town of Paradise, satisfying the significant segment 
prong. 
Regulation 18703(c) provides that a "unique effect" on a public official's financial interest 
includes a disproportionate effect on: 
(l) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the 
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity 
of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from 
the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the 
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size 
when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the 
person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family. 
As mentioned, the proposed sewer assessment district will impact approximately 90% of the 
commercial real properties within the Town of Paradise. While Mayor Jones and Councilmember 
Lotter each have a real property interest in the proposed district, there is no evidence to suggest that 
their interests will experience a disproportionate or unique effect. 


File No. A-16-175 
Page No. 6 
However, Councilmember Bolin owns two commercial properties that are professional 
office complexes, one of which consists of ten buildings with a total of 27,729 square feet, and the 
other which consists of six buildings with a total of 26,452 square feet. In addition, he owns 
commercial property containing a mini storage business that consists of 367 storage units for a total 
of 57,870 square feet on 12 acres. Because Paradise is a relatively small town,6 it would appear that 
the cumulative effect on his multiple real property interests, which includes several commercial 
buildings on each parcel, will be substantially greater than the effect on the real property interests of 
those persons, such as Mayor Jones or Councilmember Lotter, who have only a single interest. And 
you have provided no facts to indicate otherwise. Therefore, the "public generally" exception would 
not apply to Councilmember Bolin. 
Accordingly, because the public generally exception applies to Mayor Jones and 
Councilmember Lotter, both may participate in the decisions related to the proposed sewer 
assessment district. Councilmember Bolin may not, however, participate in such decisions. 
Exception 
An official is not making, participating in making or influencing a governmental decision if 
he or she makes an appearance "as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of 
its prescribed governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the 
official's personal interest . . .. " (Regulation 18704(d)(2).) The exception applies to real property 
"owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and 
members of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 18704(d)(2)(A).) 
This exception is interpreted narrowly and does not allow an official to provide general 
comments regarding the matter. Care should be taken to clarify that the official is not acting in an 
official capacity. (McHugh Ad1•ice Leiter, No. 1-98-324; Gallagher Advice Letter, No. 1-94-279 and 
Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151.) Thus, the exception does not permit an official to represent 
any other person's interests or the interests of any group. 
To qualify for the exception, an official must follow the procedures set forth in Regulation 
18707. The official must publicly identify each type of financial interest involved in the decision 
that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest. The identification must be made after the 
announcement of the agenda item to be discussed or voted on, but before the discussion or vote 
commences. The official must also recuse himself or herself from voting on the matter, leave the 
dais to speak from the same area as the members of the public and limit his or her remarks solely to 
the official's personal interests. 
6 The population is estimated to be about 26,000. (See ht1ps:/1www.townofparadise.com.) 


JW:jgl 
File No. A-16-175 
Page No. 7 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
r Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit three members of the Paradise Town 
Council from participating in decisions concerning the fonnation of a proposed sewer assessment 
district?

As explained below, although all three members have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the Act, the public generally exception applies to allow two of the members to participate in 
such decisions.

when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit three members of the Paradise Town 
Council from participating in decisions concerning the fonnation of a proposed sewer assessment 
district?

CONCLUSION: As explained below, although all three members have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the Act, the public generally exception applies to allow two of the members to participate in 
such decisions.
